content.text:
Arithmetic functions map positive integers to complex numbers. The 
most important ones are multiplicative.

content.title: Multiplicative Function
content.content:
An arithmetic function $f$ is **multiplicative** if $f(1) = 1$ and 
$f(mn) = f(m)f(n)$ whenever $\gcd(m, n) = 1$.

It is **completely multiplicative** if $f(mn) = f(m)f(n)$ for all 
$m, n$.

content.title: Standard Arithmetic Functions
content.content:
- $\tau(n)$: number of divisors of $n$

- $\sigma(n)$: sum of divisors of $n$

- $\sigma_k(n)$: sum of $k$th powers of divisors: $\sigma_k(n) = \sum_{d \mid n} d^k$

- $\phi(n)$: Euler's phi function

- $\mu(n)$: Möbius function (see below)

For a prime power $p^e$, the divisors are $1, p, p^2, \ldots, p^e$—there 
are $e + 1$ of them, summing to $(p^{e+1} - 1)/(p - 1)$.

By multiplicativity, $\tau(mn) = \tau(m)\tau(n)$ and $\sigma(mn) = \sigma(m)\sigma(n)$ 
for coprime $m, n$, giving the formulas.

content.title: Perfect Number
content.content:
A positive integer $n$ is **perfect** if $\sigma(n) = 2n$—i.e., 
$n$ equals the sum of its proper divisors.

content.title: Euclid-Euler Theorem
content.content:
$n$ is an even perfect number if and only if $n = 2^{p-1}(2^p - 1)$ 
where $2^p - 1$ is a Mersenne prime.
content.label: Theorem
